---
title: "R Base Overview"
author: ""
date: ""
output: 
  prettydoc::html_pretty:
    theme: cayman
    highlight: github
---

![](../hanze/hanze.png)

---

[Go back to the main page](../index.html)  
[Go back to the R overview page](../R/R_01_index.html)  

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE} 
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE) 
```

This Rmd file can be downloaded [here](./R_00_base_overview.Rmd)

Unlike the tidyverse, base R needs no extra packages — everything below uses functions that ship with R by default.

# 📘 Base R Overview {#base-r-overview}

💡 *This page provides a structured overview of core base R functions, covering the same operations as the [Tidyverse Overview](./R_00_tidy_overview.html) page, but without relying on any external packages. While this overview includes references and examples using base R for completeness and historical context, for modern data science workflows, the use of the Tidyverse suite of packages (such as dplyr, tidyr, and ggplot2) is strongly recommended and advised. While base R is powerful, its syntax can occasionally be inconsistent—such as automatically dropping dimensions when subsetting single columns. The Tidyverse avoids these pitfalls by maintaining predictable data structures (like tibbles) and utilizing a clean, pipe-oriented (%>% or |>) syntax. This approach significantly enhances code readability, makes your scripts easier to maintain, and ensures a more consistent programming experience.
Click a category to expand and jump to detailed examples.*

The examples shown are performed on the Puromycin dataset. The Puromycin dataset is a classic example dataset built into the base R installation (within the datasets package). It originates from a biochemical experiment and is widely used for demonstrating and testing nonlinear regression models, particularly the Michaelis-Menten kinetics model in enzyme reactions.

```{r}
head(Puromycin)
```


---

## Summary Table

💡 *This section summarizes the core base R operations on data frames. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>🧩 Row Operations</strong> — `na.omit()`, `subset()`/`[ ]`, `order()`, `unique()`, `sample()`, `which.max()`/`which.min()`</summary>

Select, subset, or reorder rows.  
➡️ [See Row Actions ↓](#row-operations)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>📊 Column Operations</strong> — `[ , ]`, `names()`, `transform()`, column reordering, `sapply()`/`lapply()`</summary>

Choose, rename, or transform columns.  
➡️ [See Column Actions ↓](#column-operations)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>🧮 Grouping & Aggregation</strong> — `split()`, `aggregate()`, `ave()`, `tapply()`</summary>

Split data into groups and compute summaries.  
➡️ [See Group Actions ↓](#grouping)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>🔗 Joins & Binding</strong> — `merge()`, `rbind()`, `cbind()`</summary>

Combine multiple data frames.  
➡️ [See Join Actions ↓](#joins)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>🔄 Reshaping Data</strong> — `reshape()`</summary>

Restructure data between long and wide formats.  
➡️ [See Reshaping ↓](#reshaping)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>📈 Visualization</strong> — `plot()`, `points()`, `lines()`, `legend()`</summary>

Create and customize plots.  
➡️ [See Visualization ↓](#visualization)
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>🧰 Utilities</strong> — `str()`, `$`/`[[ ]]`, `table()`</summary>

Inspect or extract data quickly.  
➡️ [See Utilities ↓](#utilities)
</details>

---


# Function-Specific Actions with Examples

---

## 🧩 Actions on Rows {#row-operations}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists core row operations in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>na.omit()</strong> – Drop rows containing `NA` values</summary>

First create a copy with an `NA` value: 

```{r}
Puro_with_NA <- Puromycin # create a copy
Puro_with_NA[1, 2] <- NA # replace row 1, column 2 with NA
Puro_with_NA
```

And drop the row with the NA:  

```{r}
# Drop row with NA
na.omit(Puro_with_NA)
```


</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>subset() / [ ]</strong> – Subset rows based on conditions</summary>

```{r}
# Keep only rows where rate > 200
subset(Puromycin, rate > 200)
```

Selects rows that meet logical conditions. The equivalent indexing form is `Puromycin[Puromycin$rate > 200, ]`. Supports multiple conditions with `&` (and) or `|` (or).  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>[ ]</strong> – Select rows by position</summary>

```{r}
# Keep the first 5 rows
Puromycin[1:5, ]
```

Select rows by numeric positions, e.g. with `head()`, `tail()`, or a vector of row indices.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>order()</strong> – Reorder rows by variable(s)</summary>

```{r}
# Sort by rate descending
Puromycin[order(-Puromycin$rate), ]
```

Orders rows by one or more columns. Use a minus sign (or `decreasing = TRUE`) for descending order.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>unique()</strong> – Keep unique rows</summary>

```{r}
# Keep unique values of rate
unique(Puromycin["rate"])
```

Removes duplicate rows based on one or more columns.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>sample()</strong> – Random sample of rows</summary>

```{r}
# Randomly sample 3 rows
Puromycin[sample(nrow(Puromycin), 3), ]
```

Randomly selects rows; sample a fraction by basing `size` on `round(nrow(Puromycin) * prop)`.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>order() + head()</strong> – Top or bottom rows by value</summary>

```{r}
# 3 rows with highest rate
head(Puromycin[order(-Puromycin$rate), ], 3)
```

Select rows with minimum or maximum values of a variable by ordering first, then taking the head or tail.  
</details>

---

## 📊 Actions on Columns {#column-operations}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists core column operations in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>[ , c() ]</strong> – Choose specific columns</summary>

```{r}
# Select columns conc and rate
Puromycin[, c("conc", "rate")]
```

Keeps only the specified columns. Helper functions like `grepl()` on `names(Puromycin)` can emulate `starts_with()`, `ends_with()`, or `contains()`.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>names()</strong> – Rename columns</summary>

```{r}
# Rename conc to conc_ppm
Puromycin_renamed <- Puromycin
names(Puromycin_renamed)[names(Puromycin_renamed) == "conc"] <- "conc_ppm"
Puromycin_renamed
```

Changes column names without altering the data. A copy is made first so the original `Puromycin` data frame stays untouched.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>transform()</strong> – Add or modify columns</summary>

```{r}
# Add a new column rate_per_conc
transform(Puromycin, rate_per_conc = rate / conc)
```

Creates new columns or modifies existing ones using expressions, returning a new data frame.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>data.frame()</strong> – Create new columns and drop others</summary>

```{r}
# Keep only rate_per_conc column
data.frame(rate_per_conc = Puromycin$rate / Puromycin$conc)
```

Generates a new data frame containing only the computed column(s), dropping everything else.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>column reordering</strong> – Move columns to new positions</summary>

```{r}
# Move state column to the first position
Puromycin[, c("state", setdiff(names(Puromycin), "state"))]
```

Reorders columns by re-indexing with a reordered vector of column names, without changing the data.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>sapply() / lapply()</strong> – Apply functions to multiple columns</summary>

```{r}
# Convert all numeric columns to log scale
Puromycin_log <- Puromycin
numeric_cols <- sapply(Puromycin_log, is.numeric)
Puromycin_log[numeric_cols] <- lapply(Puromycin_log[numeric_cols], log)
Puromycin_log
```

Applies a function across selected columns; `sapply()` identifies which columns qualify, and `lapply()` applies the transformation.  
</details>

---

## 🧮 Actions on Groups {#grouping}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists core grouping operations in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>split()</strong> – Group data by one or more variables</summary>

```{r}
# Group by conc
split(Puromycin, Puromycin$conc)
```

Splits the data frame into a list of data frames, one per group, for subsequent manipulation.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>do.call(rbind, ...)</strong> – Remove grouping</summary>

```{r}
# Recombine the grouped list back into a single data frame
do.call(rbind, split(Puromycin, Puromycin$conc))
```

Base R has no persistent "grouped" object the way tibbles do — a list created by `split()` is just recombined with `rbind()` when grouping is no longer needed.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>aggregate()</strong> – Compute summary statistics by group</summary>

```{r}
# Average rate by conc
aggregate(rate ~ conc, data = Puromycin, FUN = mean)
```

Aggregates data within each group to produce summary statistics, similar to `group_by() %>% summarize()`.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>ave()</strong> – Add or modify columns within groups</summary>

```{r}
# Compute rank of rate within each conc group
transform(Puromycin, rank_rate = ave(rate, conc, FUN = rank))
```

`ave()` computes a group-wise statistic and recycles it back to the original row count, making it the base R equivalent of `mutate()` after `group_by()`.  
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>ave() + subsetting</strong> – Subset rows within groups</summary>

```{r}
# Keep the row(s) with the top rate within each conc group
Puromycin[Puromycin$rate == ave(Puromycin$rate, Puromycin$conc, FUN = max), ]
```

Allows row selection within each group by comparing each row's value to a group-wise statistic computed with `ave()`.  
</details>

---

## 🔗 Joins & Bindings {#joins}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists core joining operations in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>merge()</strong> – A join combines rows from two data frames based on a common key.</summary>

```{r}
# Create a copy of puromycin
puromycin <- Puromycin
head(puromycin)
```

```{r}
# Create a second dataset with additional info
treatment_info <- data.frame(
  state = c("treated", "untreated"),
  description = c("Received Puromycin", "Control group")
)
treatment_info
```


```{r}
# Left join: add treatment descriptions
merge(puromycin, treatment_info, by = "state", all.x = TRUE, sort = FALSE)
```

Thus:  

- `merge(puromycin, treatment_info, by = "state", all.x = TRUE)` merges the description column into the puromycin dataset by matching the state column.  
- `all.x = TRUE` keeps every row in `puromycin` (a left join); without it, `merge()` would default to an inner join.  

</details>


<details>
<summary><strong>rbind() / cbind()</strong> – Binding combines data frames either by rows (`rbind()`) or by columns (`cbind()`).</summary>

```{r}
# Split Puromycin into two groups
treated <- subset(puromycin, state == "treated")
untreated <- subset(puromycin, state == "untreated")

treated # Show treated, 12 rows
```

```{r}
# Row binding: recombine after filtering
rbind(treated, untreated) # now 23 rows
```



```{r}
# Column binding: add an extra column
extra_info <- data.frame(source = rep("Lab A", nrow(puromycin)))
extra_info
```

```{r}
cbind(puromycin, extra_info) # Columns bound
```

Thus:  
- `rbind()` is for stacking datasets vertically (more rows).  
- `cbind()` is for combining datasets horizontally (more columns).  

</details>

---

## 🔄 Reshaping Data {#reshaping}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists restructure operations of data between long and wide formats in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>reshape() to wide</strong> – Spread key-value pairs across multiple columns. This is useful when you want to separate values into distinct columns.</summary>

First create an extra column with replicates:  

```{r}
Puromycin_reps <- Puromycin
Puromycin_reps$replicate <- rep(1:2, length.out = nrow(Puromycin_reps))
Puromycin_reps
```

Now make the data wide:  

```{r}
Puromycin_wide <- reshape(Puromycin_reps, idvar = c("conc", "state"),
                           timevar = "replicate", direction = "wide")
Puromycin_wide
```

Note that base R's `reshape()` names the new columns by pasting the value column with the `timevar` (e.g. `rate.1`, `rate.2`), whereas `pivot_wider()` names them directly after the `replicate` values (`1`, `2`). The underlying restructuring is the same.

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>reshape() to long</strong> – Convert columns into key-value pairs. This is useful when you want to gather multiple columns into a single column.</summary>

```{r}
Puromycin_long <- reshape(Puromycin_wide, direction = "long")
Puromycin_long
```

</details>

---

## 📈 Visualization {#visualization}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section shows how to plot with base R graphics. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>plot()</strong> - The function `plot()` is used to create visualizations of your data.</summary>

It works with the following layers:

1. Initialization Layer 
2. Geometry Layer 
3. Statistical Layer 
4. Scale Layer 
5. Label Layer 
6. Theme Layer 

Base R has no explicit "layer" system like ggplot2 — instead, a plot is built by calling `plot()` once to set up the canvas, then adding to it with functions like `points()`, `abline()`, `legend()`, and `mtext()`, or by passing more arguments to `plot()` directly. To show the same conceptual layers using the Puromycin dataset, each step below redraws the full plot with one more piece added.

Here is a step-by-step example using R code for a scatter plot with a fitted line, illustrating the key layers:  

- Initialization Layer 

Sets up the plotting region and axes, with no data drawn yet.

```{r}
plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate, type = "n", xlab = "", ylab = "")
```

As you can see, no data are plotted yet. An XY-grid is created and the axes are set.  

- Geometry Layer 

Adds the visual representation of the data (points for a scatter plot), using a color per state.

```{r}
col_map <- c(treated = 1, untreated = 2) # default base R colors

plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate,
     col = col_map[Puromycin$state],
     pch = 19, cex = 1.5,
     xlab = "", ylab = "")
```

As you can see, the data is plotted now. Instead of `pch = 19`, other values can be used for other point shapes, and `lines()` or `barplot()` can be used for other plot types.  

- Statistical Layer 

Adds a statistical summary (e.g., a line fit to the data, one per group).
For simplicity, we just use a linear model.

```{r}
col_map <- c(treated = 1, untreated = 2)

plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate,
     col = col_map[Puromycin$state],
     pch = 19, cex = 1.5,
     xlab = "", ylab = "")

for (s in names(col_map)) {
  sub <- subset(Puromycin, state == s)
  fit <- lm(rate ~ conc, data = sub)
  abline(fit, col = col_map[s], lwd = 2)
}
```

Note that for plotting enzyme activity, we normally use the Michaelis-Menten model. This requires more complexity and is omitted in this example. In this case, we used a linear model fit per group with `abline()`, for demonstration purposes.  

- Scale Layer 

Controls the mapping from the data values to the visual properties (e.g., color), made explicit here with a custom color vector and `legend()`.  

```{r}
col_map <- c(treated = "purple", untreated = "darkgreen")

plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate,
     col = col_map[Puromycin$state],
     pch = 19, cex = 1.5,
     xlab = "", ylab = "")

for (s in names(col_map)) {
  sub <- subset(Puromycin, state == s)
  fit <- lm(rate ~ conc, data = sub)
  abline(fit, col = col_map[s], lwd = 2)
}

legend("bottomright", legend = names(col_map), col = col_map, pch = 19, title = "Enzyme State")
```


- Label Layer 


Adds non-data annotations like titles, axis labels, and a caption, using `main`/`xlab`/`ylab` and `mtext()`.

```{r}
col_map <- c(treated = "purple", untreated = "darkgreen")

plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate,
     col = col_map[Puromycin$state],
     pch = 19, cex = 1.5,
     main = "Reaction Rate vs. Substrate Concentration in Puromycin",
     xlab = "Concentration (ppm)",
     ylab = "Reaction Rate (counts/min)")

for (s in names(col_map)) {
  sub <- subset(Puromycin, state == s)
  fit <- lm(rate ~ conc, data = sub)
  abline(fit, col = col_map[s], lwd = 2)
}

mtext("Effect of enzyme state (treated/untreated)", side = 3, line = 0.5, cex = 0.9)
legend("bottomright", legend = names(col_map), col = col_map, pch = 19, title = "Enzyme State")
mtext("Data from Puromycin dataset", side = 1, line = 4, adj = 1, cex = 0.7)
```

- Theme Layer
  

Controls the non-data graphical elements (e.g., background, grid lines, font size), set globally with `par()` and `grid()`.  


```{r}
col_map <- c(treated = "purple", untreated = "darkgreen")

par(bg = "white", family = "sans", cex.main = 1.2, cex.lab = 1.05, cex.axis = 0.9)

plot(Puromycin$conc, Puromycin$rate,
     col = col_map[Puromycin$state],
     pch = 19, cex = 1.5,
     main = "Reaction Rate vs. Substrate Concentration in Puromycin",
     xlab = "Concentration (ppm)",
     ylab = "Reaction Rate (counts/min)",
     panel.first = grid(col = "grey90"))

for (s in names(col_map)) {
  sub <- subset(Puromycin, state == s)
  fit <- lm(rate ~ conc, data = sub)
  abline(fit, col = col_map[s], lwd = 2)
}

mtext("Effect of enzyme state (treated/untreated)", side = 3, line = 0.5, cex = 0.9)
legend("bottomright", legend = names(col_map), col = col_map, pch = 19, title = "Enzyme State")
mtext("Data from Puromycin dataset", side = 1, line = 4, adj = 1, cex = 0.7)
```

This is just a demonstration of the use of the various layers.  
For a complete overview of base R graphics see [this](https://stat.ethz.ch/R-manual/R-devel/library/graphics/html/graphics-package.html) link.

You can also download a pdf cheatsheet [here](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/blob/main/base-r.pdf).

</details>

---

## 🧰 Utilities {#utilities}

[⬆️ Back to Summary](#base-r-overview)

💡 *This section lists some utility operations in base R. Click on the triangles to expand.*

<details>
<summary><strong>str()</strong> - The function `str()` is great for quick inspection of your data. Helps you verify column types and spot missing or unexpected values.</summary>

```{r}
str(Puromycin)
```
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>$ / [[ ]]</strong> - Used to extract a single column from a data frame as a vector.</summary>

```{r}
Puromycin$rate
```

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>table()</strong> - The function `table()` is used to tally the number of occurrences of values in one or more columns.</summary>

```{r}
table(Puromycin$state)
```


</details>

---

[Go back to the main page](../index.html)  
[Go back to the R overview page](../R/R_01_index.html)  
<a href="#top">⬆️ Back to Top</a>  

---


>This web page is distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.
>Creative Commons License: CC BY-SA 4.0.
